en.extract:
The restinga tyrannulet (Phylloscartes kronei) is a species of bird in the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. It is endemic to Brazil.


Taxonomy and systematics
The restinga tyrannulet is monotypic.
The first known specimen of the restinga tyrannulet was collected in 1898 but was misidentified as a mottle-cheeked tyrannulet (P. ventralis). Willis and Oniki corrected the error in their formal description of the new species. They assigned the specific epithet kronei in honor of Ricardo Krone, the Brazilian zoologist who collected the 1898 specimen.


Description
The restinga tyrannulet is about 12 cm (4.7 in) long and weighs 7.25 to 10 g (0.26 to 0.35 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a thin yellow supercilium, a yellow broken eye-ring, and a dusky line through the eye that continues to the rear and sweeps around the mottled dusky and yellowish ear coverts. Their crown, nape, back, and rump are greenish olive that is slightly browner on the crown. Their wings are dusky with yellowish outer webs on the flight feathers. Their wing coverts have yellow tips that form two wing bars. Their tail is dusky olive with thin greenish edges on the feathers. Their chin and throat are pale yellow. The rest of their underparts are medium yellow that is brightest on the belly and has faint greenish mottling on the breast and flanks. Both sexes have a brown iris, a black bill with a pale base to the mandible, and gray legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
The restinga tyrannulet is found in southeastern Brazil along a narrow coastal band from the floodplain of the Ribeira River in southern São Paulo state south into northeastern Rio Grande do Sul. Its primary habitat is restinga, which is low-stature woodlands on coastal sand ridges. It also occurs in adjacent evergreen forest, secondary forest, and riverside forest. In elevation it is found almost entirely very near sea level but locally occurs as high as 600 m (2,000 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The restinga tyrannulet is a year-round resident.


Feeding
The restinga tyrannulet feeds primarily on arthropods and also includes fruits in its diet. It forages actively, usually from the forest's understory up to about 15 m (50 ft) above the ground. It typically perches on a sloping branch and makes short sallies to grab prey from leaves and twigs. It typically forages singly or in pairs and rarely joins mixed-species feeding flocks.


Breeding
The restinga tyrannulet breeds between September and January. Females take about 19 days to build the nest, a closed ball of moss and spider web lined with dry grass and seed fluff. It typically is in or suspended from a branch fork between about 0.7 and 3 m (2 and 10 ft) above the ground. The clutch size is two or three eggs. Females alone incubate, for about 12 days, though males remain near the nest. The time to fledging is not known. Both parents provision nestlings.


Vocalization
The restinga tyrannulet's song is a "very high, fast, twittering series" written as sit-it-it-it-it-it-it-it-it-it-sitit-sitit. Its call is a "very high 'swee' ".


Status
The IUCN originally in 1994 assessed the restinga tyrannulet as Vulnerable and since August 2019 as being of Least Concern. Its range is estimated at 58,500 km2 (22,600 sq mi) but it is thought to actually occupy only about 5,400 km2 (2,100 sq mi) within that range. Its estimated population of between 2500 and 10,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. "Within its range, suitable habitat is rapidly cleared for beachfront dwellings and tourist developments, notably on Ilha Comprida, and future pressure on restingas are likely to be great. Deliberately or accidentally started fires are also a threat." The species is known to occur in at least 11 protected areas.


References


External links
Restinga tyrannulet photo gallery VIREO

The '''restinga tyrannulet''' ('''''Phylloscartes kronei''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Tyrannidae]], the [[tyrant flycatcher]]s. It is [[endemic]] to [[Brazil]].<ref name=IOC14.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.2 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=August 2024 |access-date=19 August 2024 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The restinga tyrannulet is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC14.2/>

The first known specimen of the restinga tyrannulet was collected in 1898 but was misidentified as a [[mottle-cheeked tyrannulet]] (''P. ventralis''). Willis and Oniki corrected the error in their [[Species description|formal description]] of the new species. They assigned the [[specific epithet]] ''kronei'' in honor of Ricardo Krone, the Brazilian zoologist who collected the 1898 specimen.<ref name=Willis>{{cite journal |last1=Willis |first1=Edwin O'Neill |last2=Oniki |first2=Yoshika | journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club | title=A new ''Phylloscartes'' (Tyrannidae) from southeastern Brazil | volume=112 |issue=3 | pages=158–165 | publisher=British Ornithologists' Club | date= 1992 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40027911 }}</ref>

==Description==

The restinga tyrannulet is about {{convert|12|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|7.25|to|10|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a thin yellow [[supercilium]], a yellow broken [[eye-ring]], and a dusky line through the eye that continues to the rear and sweeps around the mottled dusky and yellowish ear [[coverts]]. Their crown, nape, back, and rump are greenish olive that is slightly browner on the crown. Their wings are dusky with yellowish outer webs on the flight feathers. Their wing [[coverts]] have yellow tips that form two [[wing bar]]s. Their tail is dusky olive with thin greenish edges on the feathers. Their chin and throat are pale yellow. The rest of their underparts are medium yellow that is brightest on the belly and has faint greenish mottling on the breast and flanks. Both sexes have a brown iris, a black bill with a pale base to the [[mandible]], and gray legs and feet.<ref name=Willis/><ref name=RETY-BOW>Gussoni, C. O. (2024). Restinga Tyrannulet (''Phylloscartes kronei''), version 3.0. In Birds of the World (T. S. Schulenberg, B. K. Keeney, and S. M. Billerman, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.restyr1.03 retrieved December 20, 2024</ref><ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | pages =300–301 | isbn =978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The restinga tyrannulet is found in southeastern Brazil along a narrow coastal band from the floodplain of the [[Ribeira de Iguape River|Ribeira River]] in southern [[São Paulo (state)|São Paulo]] state south into northeastern [[Rio Grande do Sul]]. Its primary habitat is ''[[Atlantic Coast restingas|restinga]]'', which is low-stature woodlands on coastal sand ridges. It also occurs in adjacent [[evergreen forest]], [[secondary forest]], and riverside forest. In elevation it is found almost entirely very near sea level but locally occurs as high as {{convert|600|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Willis/><ref name=RETY-BOW/><ref name=vanPerlo/><ref name=BLI>{{cite web |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/restinga-tyrannulet-phylloscartes-kronei |title=Restinga Tyrannulet ''Phylloscartes kronei'' |display-authors=et al | author=Sharpe, C. J. |date=2024 |website=BirdLife International |access-date=December 20, 2024 }}</ref>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The restinga tyrannulet is a year-round resident.<ref name=RETY-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The restinga tyrannulet feeds primarily on [[arthropod]]s and also includes fruits in its diet. It forages actively, usually from the forest's understory up to about {{convert|15|m|ft|round=5|abbr=on}} above the ground. It typically perches on a sloping branch and makes short sallies to grab prey from leaves and twigs. It typically forages singly or in pairs and rarely joins [[mixed-species feeding flock]]s.<ref name=RETY-BOW/><ref name=BLI/>

===Breeding===

The restinga tyrannulet breeds between September and January. Females take about 19 days to build the nest, a closed ball of moss and spider web lined with dry grass and seed fluff. It typically is in or suspended from a branch fork between about {{convert|0.7|and|3|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} above the ground. The clutch size is two or three eggs. Females alone incubate, for about 12 days, though males remain near the nest. The time to fledging is not known. Both parents provision nestlings.<ref name=RETY-BOW/><ref name=BLI/><ref>Gussoni, C. O. A., and M. A. Pizo (2018). "Breeding biology of the Restinga Tyrannulet (''Phylloscartes kronei'')". ''Wilson Journal of Ornithology'' 130:591–599.</ref>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Phylloscartes-kronei |species=the restinga tyrannulet}}
===Vocalization===

The restinga tyrannulet's song is a "very high, fast, twittering series"<ref name=vanPerlo/> written as ''sit-it-it-it-it-it-it-it-it-it-sitit-sitit''<ref name=Willis/><!-- -->. Its call is a "very high 'swee' ".<ref name=vanPerlo/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] originally in 1994 assessed the restinga tyrannulet as Vulnerable and since August 2019 as being of Least Concern. Its range is estimated at {{convert|58,500|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} but it is thought to actually occupy only about {{convert|5400|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} within that range. Its estimated population of between 2500 and 10,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. "Within its range, suitable habitat is rapidly cleared for beachfront dwellings and tourist developments, notably on [[Ilha Comprida]], and future pressure on restingas are likely to be great. Deliberately or accidentally started fires are also a threat."<ref name=IUCN/> The species is known to occur in at least 11 protected areas.<ref name=BLI/>
